questions.question: Approximate the area under $f(x) = x^2$ on $[0,2]$ using a left Riemann sum with 4 subintervals.
Approximate the area under $f(x) = 2x + 1$ on $[0,3]$ using a right Riemann sum with 3 subintervals.

Given the table below, use a left Riemann sum with 3 subintervals to estimate $\int_{0}^{6} f(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} x & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\ \hline f(x) & 3 & 5 & 2 & 4 \end{array}$
Use a midpoint Riemann sum with 2 subintervals to approximate $\int_{0}^{4} \sqrt{x} \, dx$.
The velocity of a car (m/s) is recorded every 2 seconds: $t=0$: 0, $t=2$: 10, $t=4$: 18, $t=6$: 24. Use a right Riemann sum to estimate the distance traveled from $t=0$ to $t=6$.
questions.explanation: $\Delta x = 2$; right endpoints: $v(2) = 10$, $v(4) = 18$, $v(6) = 24$. Sum = 52, times 2 = 104 m.

questions.question: Approximate $\int_{1}^{5} \frac{1}{x} \, dx$ using a left Riemann sum with $n = 4$ equal subintervals.
For $f(x) = 4 - x^2$ on $[-2,2]$, use a midpoint Riemann sum with 4 subintervals to approximate the area.

The function $f$ is given by the table. Use a right Riemann sum with 3 subintervals to estimate $\int_{2}^{11} f(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} x & 2 & 5 & 8 & 11 \\ \hline f(x) & 6 & 3 & 7 & 4 \end{array}$
Approximate the area under $f(x) = \sin x$ on $[0,\pi]$ using a left Riemann sum with 3 subintervals.
A particle's velocity (ft/s) is given by $v(t) = t^2$. Use a midpoint Riemann sum with 4 subintervals to estimate the displacement from $t=0$ to $t=4$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered Riemann sum approximations.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! You understand the basics of Riemann sums. Review midpoint calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on identifying endpoints and calculating $\Delta x$ correctly.
scoreMessages.0: Review the definition of Riemann sums: $\sum f(x_i^*) \Delta x$.
